conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.
I can understand why you'd feel that way, given how many times you've tried quitting before. What is one thing from those past attempts that you think might actually help you this time around?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

It sounds like you're really struggling with the fear of gaining weight and feeling like you've tried everything before. What do you think is the biggest reason that's holding you back from trying a new approach to quit smoking?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about quitting and worried that you're not in the right mindset. Can you tell me what's holding you back from feeling ready to quit smoking?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain and unsure about making any real progress towards quitting, but at the same time, you're here and willing to explore some small steps. What would it take for you to feel more confident in your ability to take small steps towards quitting smoking?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit resistant to this process right now. Can you tell me what's behind your hesitation, is it the idea of quitting or something else entirely?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

I understand that it feels like quitting smoking is a part of your identity, and that past attempts haven't worked out for you. Can you tell me what's changed in your life that might be making you consider quitting now?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

It sounds like the previous methods you've tried have had some negative effects on your mental health and also led to weight gain, which is really tough to deal with. Can you tell me what you think might be the smallest step you could take towards quitting that wouldn't feel too overwhelming?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

It sounds like you feel misunderstood by your doctor and loved ones, and that's frustrating. How do you think your family and friends would react if you told them you're serious about quitting and asked for their support?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

You sound really discouraged, and it's understandable that past experiences can make you feel that way. Can you tell me about a time when you felt like you were making progress or feeling hopeful about quitting, even if it was just for a little while?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.

It sounds like you're willing to take a small step towards quitting, but you're still feeling cautious about making any big promises. What do you think would make you feel more confident about setting a specific goal for reducing your smoking?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.

It sounds like you're feeling really discouraged about trying to quit again, and you're not sure why you're even bothering. 

Can you tell me, what's the hardest part for you about quitting - is it the physical symptoms or something else that keeps you going back to cigarettes?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

I can see that you're really struggling with the idea of quitting, and the fear of gaining weight is a big concern for you. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you did gain weight - is it the physical changes that are making you nervous, or something else?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
It sounds like you're feeling uncertain and not motivated to quit right now. What is it about your current mindset that makes you think you're not ready to quit smoking?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?

You're open to exploring small steps, but you're not feeling committed yet. What if we start with creating a reward system for yourself - something to look forward to once you reach certain milestones, like staying smoke-free for a day or a week?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

The patient seems to be saying they're reluctantly agreeing to move forward with small steps towards quitting, but still hasn't committed fully to the process. 

How do you think we can build on this small step of agreeing to look at your daily routine and identify your triggers for smoking?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

It sounds like you're feeling quite disconnected from the idea of quitting, and like smoking is a big part of your identity. Can you tell me what is the most important thing you're afraid to give up by quitting smoking?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

It sounds like you've tried various methods to quit smoking in the past, but unfortunately, they've led to more anxiety and depression, which is really tough to deal with. Have you noticed if your anxiety levels are particularly high when you're thinking about quitting, rather than when you're actually smoking?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.
You feel like your doctor and loved ones just don't understand how difficult quitting is for you. Can you tell me more about how it feels when they don't get it?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

I can hear the weight of past failures and feelings of hopelessness in your voice. You're right, quitting isn't easy, and it's normal to feel overwhelmed by your history of unsuccessful attempts. Can you tell me what it would take for you to feel hopeful about quitting again?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.

It sounds like you're feeling hesitant and uncertain about committing to quitting. What's holding you back from making a promise to yourself right now to take small steps towards reducing your smoking?

Honestly, I'm feeling really frustrated. I've been trying to quit for weeks now, but it's just not sticking. I've been smoking for about 10 years, and I know it's not good for me, but it's hard to stop.

It sounds like quitting is really tough for you right now, and you're feeling frustrated about not being able to stick with it. 

What specifically is making it hard for you to stop smoking this time?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.

It sounds like your love and concern for your niece and nephew are a powerful motivator for you to quit smoking. Can you think of a way to celebrate your successes, like staying smoke-free for a day or a week, to help you feel more positive and encouraged?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
Rachel, it sounds like you're feeling uncertain about your ability to quit, partly because of past failed attempts. Can you tell me more about what made those attempts harder for you?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.

It sounds like having a supportive environment is really crucial for you, Rachel. Can you think of anyone you'd be comfortable talking to about your struggles with quitting, like a friend or family member who might be willing to join you in quitting or at least be supportive of your efforts?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.
You're feeling unsure about seeking outside help, Rachel. What's holding you back from giving it a try?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
You're considering trying some new resources to help with quitting. What specifically are you hoping to gain from joining a support group or talking to a counselor?

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.

I can sense your uncertainty and concern about the cost of quitting, but first, let's talk about what motivated you to come here today - what's been going on that made you decide to try and quit again?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

I can sense how worried you are about the cost of quitting and the fear of failure. That can be really overwhelming. Have you talked to your healthcare provider or insurance company about what options might be available to help make quitting more affordable?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.

The patient is acknowledging that they understand their nicotine dependence level, and they're interested in learning about the available options to help them quit smoking. 

What would you say are some non-pharmacological strategies that could help you manage cravings and stay motivated when trying to quit smoking?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

You're considering trying nicotine replacement therapy, which is a great first step. How do you think you'll manage the financial aspects of purchasing and using nicotine replacement therapy, such as finding a way to afford the copays or navigating your insurance coverage?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.

The patient seems to be feeling more hopeful and confident about quitting. They're expressing gratitude and feeling positive about their ability to succeed this time. 

What are some specific steps you'd like to take in the next few days to start reducing your smoking?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.

You're feeling pretty confident about your smoking, and it's not something that's been causing you major concerns. How long has it been since you've even thought about the potential risks associated with smoking?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?

You're feeling attached to the social aspect of smoking and think it's part of your identity. 

How do you think your life would change if you were able to find new ways to connect with friends and have fun without smoking?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.
I hear that you're worried about weight gain and losing a part of your social experience when you quit smoking. Can you tell me more about what you're afraid of when it comes to weight gain?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

I understand that you feel like quitting would mean giving up a big part of your identity as a bartender and losing connection with your friends. Can you think of any ways you might still be able to connect with your friends and be part of the culture without smoking?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.
You're feeling uncertain and unsure about quitting, and that's okay. What's holding you back from wanting to quit right now, is it the fear of missing out on social experiences or something else?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.

It sounds like you're feeling really frustrated with yourself about starting again, and it's understandable why you'd feel that way. What was it that made you think you wanted to quit in the first place, even if it hasn't stuck yet?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

It sounds like you were making progress and feeling confident about quitting, but then stress from work triggered a relapse. What specifically about the stress at work made you feel like you needed a cigarette?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?

The patient is expressing uncertainty and curiosity about nicotine replacement therapy and prescription medications. I want to help them understand these options better.

Can you tell me what's holding you back from trying nicotine replacement therapy or prescription medications, if anything?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.

It sounds like you're considering the idea of using nicotine replacement therapy or prescription medications, which is a positive step towards reducing your smoking. What are your thoughts about using these options in conjunction with some of the stress-reducing strategies we discussed earlier?
Yeah, that would be great.

You're open to learning about healthy habits to manage potential weight gain. That's a great first step towards making positive changes.

What do you think are some foods or activities you could start with to help you feel more confident in your ability to stay healthy while quitting smoking?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.
You're feeling hopeful about cutting down to one cigarette break a day, that's a good first step. What's making you think that this is a realistic goal, and how do you plan to stick to it?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.

The patient is expressing a sense of willingness to accept help and support, which is a great step forward. What specific challenges do you think you'll face in the next week as you try to cut down to one cigarette break a day, and how can we prepare for those together?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.

It sounds like you're really struggling with feeling frustrated and disappointed in yourself over your attempts to quit smoking. 

What do you think was the smallest thing that you managed to change or control during your previous attempts to quit, even if it was just for a short time?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

It sounds like smoking has become a way to cope with your depression and anxiety, and that makes it even harder to quit. Can you tell me more about what you think you'd gain from quitting that would help you manage your depression and anxiety?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

It sounds like smoking is acting as a temporary escape for you when you're feeling anxious or overwhelmed. Can you imagine what it would be like to find other ways to calm down and manage those feelings, rather than turning to smoking?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

You're starting to see that quitting smoking might be just one part of a bigger puzzle. Can you imagine what it might be like to have a support system in place, like a therapist or counselor, to help you deal with those tough emotions and feelings of overwhelm?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about quitting and overwhelmed with other things going on in your life. Can you tell me a little bit about what's been going on that's making it hard for you to think about quitting right now?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit overwhelmed with everything going on in your life, and quitting smoking might feel like one more thing to worry about. 

Can you tell me which of these feels more manageable to you right now: quitting smoking or dealing with the other challenges in your life?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.

You're saying that even though you've heard about the benefits of quitting, you're not entirely convinced that it's true for you. What's one thing that might make you question your current health status and make you more open to the idea of quitting smoking?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.

The patient is expressing concern that quitting smoking might exacerbate their depression and anxiety symptoms. 

Are you worried that quitting smoking will make it harder for your medication to work, or that you'll have to adjust your medication in some way?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.

The patient seems to be cautiously optimistic about the idea of quitting smoking and working on managing their depression and anxiety. Can you tell me what's been going through your mind since we talked about the potential benefits of quitting smoking?
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.

You've mentioned gaining weight as a concern, and it's a common fear for many people who quit smoking. How do you think we can work together to find healthy ways to manage your weight while quitting smoking?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit more open to exploring ways to quit smoking, Alex. What do you think would help you feel more motivated to stay on track with your quit goal?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.

You've been smoking for 15 years and have tried to quit three times before, which is actually quite impressive. What was it about those previous attempts that made you think they were successful, even if they didn't last long?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.

You're really motivated to quit for the sake of your daughter and your own health, and that's a beautiful thing. What do you think would happen if your daughter found out you were quitting and was actually supporting you in that process?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit isolated in your quit journey, and that's really tough. Who in your life could be that person for you, someone who understands what you're going through and can offer you support when you need it?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.

You're feeling a bit disconnected from the support you're getting online, and it's not really working for you. What is one thing that you wish you could get from a support system that you're not getting right now?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

You're sharing with me that cravings are really tough for you, especially in situations like stressful work meetings or when you're driving. How do you think you would feel if you were able to get through just one of those situations without giving in to a craving?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
I can see you're really committed to quitting this time, and that's a huge step forward. What do you think is the most important thing for you to remember when those intense cravings hit you?

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.

It sounds like you're feeling really stuck and discouraged after relapsing into your old smoking habits. What do you think is the most challenging part about resisting the urge to smoke when you're in social situations?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

It sounds like being around your friends and drinking triggers your urge to smoke, making it feel like a big part of your social identity. How do you think your friends would react if you told them you're trying to quit smoking?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

I can see that you're feeling anxious about quitting and worried that it might make social situations more awkward. How do you think your friends would react if you told them you're quitting and asked for their support?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

It sounds like you're feeling discouraged and like you don't want to try again because of past failures. Do you think it might be helpful to reframe those past attempts as learning experiences, rather than failures, and see if we can find anything from those attempts that you could use to help you this time around?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

The patient seems unsure about quitting and values smoking as a way to relax. 

Can you tell me more about what you mean by "it's just part of who I am"? What do you think that would change about you if you were to quit smoking?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.

You're saying that you've been smoking in response to stress at work, to break the monotony of waiting for a job to start, and to feel more relaxed in social situations. What do you think would happen if, instead of reaching for a cigarette in those moments, you took a short walk outside or practiced some deep breathing exercises?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

Alex, it sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed with the idea of quitting smoking, and that it's connected to other challenges in your life. It's also clear that you're not ready to quit using substances like drinking and pot yet. Can you tell me more about what you think you'd need to feel ready to make some changes in your life?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.

The patient is expressing skepticism about the potential benefits of quitting smoking, while still being somewhat open to discussing the idea. 

Can you tell me what specifically makes you think that quitting smoking wouldn't make a huge difference in your life?

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.

I can sense the frustration and worry in your voice, and it's completely normal to feel that way. What do you think helped even a little bit when you tried quitting in the past, like with cold turkey or nicotine gum?

Well, I know it's bad for my health, and I'm worried about the impact it's having on my lungs and heart. But to be honest, it's also the stress and anxiety that comes with it. I use tobacco as a way to cope with my emotions, and I'm not sure what I'll do without it. I'm afraid that quitting will just make things worse.

It sounds like you're feeling anxious about giving up tobacco because it's been a way for you to cope with stress and emotions. Can you think of any other ways you might have managed stress before you started smoking?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.

I can sense the weight of your struggles, and it takes a lot of courage to admit them. You've been using nicotine gum, but it seems like it's not quite working for you. How are you feeling about the idea of trying something else to manage your cravings?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.

It sounds like you're really struggling with the social aspect of smoking and missing the ritual of it. How do you think you could find alternative ways to enjoy social situations and relax without smoking?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.
It sounds like you're really concerned about how quitting smoking might affect your depression and anxiety. Have you talked to your doctor or therapist about this before, and what did they say?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.

The patient feels stuck and unsure about how to move forward with quitting smoking. What's one thing you're hoping to get out of our therapy sessions that would help you feel more confident and in control?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.

It sounds like quitting has been really tough for you, and you're feeling discouraged about it. 

What's going through your mind when you think about quitting again, is it the same thing that made it hard for you last time?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.

It sounds like social situations, especially when offered cigarettes by friends, are a big trigger for you. Can you think of one thing you could say to politely decline the offer of a cigarette the next time it happens?

I know it's bad for me, and I hate the way it makes me feel... guilty, anxious, and out of control. But at the same time, I really enjoy smoking. It's hard to explain, but it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation when we're together.

You're acknowledging that you both enjoy and struggle with smoking, and that being around friends who smoke makes it hard for you to resist. How do you think you could find alternative ways to feel relaxed and happy with your friends without smoking?
I guess so... I just don't know if I can do it. I feel like I've tried everything already.
I hear your uncertainty and frustration, but what's one small thing you think might make a difference in your journey to quitting or reducing smoking, something you haven't tried before?
Yeah, okay. I guess that sounds like a good place to start.

It sounds like you're feeling more open to exploring some new strategies, which is a great step forward. What specifically are you hoping to achieve by starting to address your smoking in social situations, like when you're out with friends at a bar?
Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.

It sounds like you've grown accustomed to smoking as a part of your daily routine, and that seeing others smoke around you doesn't make you think twice about it. What do you think you would be most worried about if you were to stop smoking for a day?

It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.

It sounds like smoking has become a deeply ingrained habit for you, and you're worried that quitting might be hard because of your friends. Can you think of one reason why quitting smoking would be worth it to you, even if it's just a small reason right now?

You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?

You're worried that quitting will affect your social life and friendships, especially when you're out at the bar. How do you think your friends might react if you explained to them your health concerns and your decision to quit smoking?
